Phillipines- Datapoints: AI Preparedness Index Score is 0.5

Stage 1\n Greenfield\n (No Activity): No national AI strategy or policy; absent from government agenda
Stage 2\n Open to Adopt\n (Intent / Plan: Draft strategy or formal consultations underway
Stage 3\nEarly Success\n (Strategy/frame: National AI strategy formally published
Stage 4\nMaturing\n(Implementation): AI strategy actively implemented with defined targets and allocated funding
Stage 5\n Role Model\n (Leadership and: Comprehensive, regularly reviewed AI strategy with measurable outcomes and enforcement mechanisms
Phillipines- Datapoints:
- National AI Strategy and Research Agenda 2.0 (NAISR 2.0) launched July 2024 by the Department of Trade and Industry (DTI), covering seven strategic imperatives across infrastructure, talent, and ethical AI
- Centre for AI Research (CAIR) established concurrently as the Philippines' national AI research hub, targeting agriculture and healthcare sectors
- As of 2026, implementation remains early-stage — no public monitoring framework or verified progress tracking in place (UNESCO, 2025)

Sub-Parameter: AI Governance and Regulatory Frameworks and Ethical AI Principles
Stage 1\n Greenfield\n (No Activity): No governance framework or regulatory discussion; No ethical AI discussion or principles
Stage 2\n Open to Adopt\n (Intent / Plan: Initial discussions on AI governance; no formal framework; Ethical AI principles under discussion or consultation
Stage 3\nEarly Success\n (Strategy/frame: AI governance guidelines or regulatory approach published; Ethical AI principles formally published
Stage 4\nMaturing\n(Implementation): AI governance framework operationalised; regulatory body or oversight function designated;Ethical AI principles embedded in procurement, deployment standards, and public-sector mandates
Stage 5\n Role Model\n (Leadership and: Comprehensive regulatory regime with active oversight, enforcement, and adaptive review cycles;Ethical AI framework benchmarked internationally; referenced or adopted by peer countries
Phillipines- Datapoints:
- No enacted AI-specific legislation as of 2026; Senate Bill No. 25 (Artificial Intelligence Regulation Act) remains in Senate committee
- House Bill 7396 (comprehensive AI deployment framework) also pending enactment
- DICT and Civil Service Commission published a draft Joint Memorandum Circular on Ethical AI Use in Government (April 2024) — open for public consultation; not yet enacted as of March 2026
- Draft JMC draws on OECD AI Principles, UNESCO Recommendation on Ethics of AI, and the ASEAN AI Governance Guide
- No dedicated national lead agency for AI governance currently exists (UNESCO RAM, December 2025)
Insight?:
- The Philippines is at a critical governance inflection point: AI legislation (SB 25) and ethical guidelines are being shaped NOW, with no lead agency yet designated. UNDP can step in as a neutral convenor to support the design of an inclusive AI governance architecture — drawing on its work with UNESCO's AI RAM and OECD AI Principles to ensure civil society and marginalised voices are embedded before these structures solidify.
- Opportunity to position UNDP as the preferred technical partner for the DICT in operationalising the Joint Memorandum Circular on Ethical AI Use in Government — a concrete, near-term deliverable with institutional traction.

Stage 1\n Greenfield\n (No Activity): No data protection legislation
Stage 2\n Open to Adopt\n (Intent / Plan: Draft data protection law or consultation underway
Stage 3\nEarly Success\n (Strategy/frame: Data protection legislation enacted
Stage 4\nMaturing\n(Implementation): Data protection law enforced with designated authority and complaint mechanisms
Stage 5\n Role Model\n (Leadership and: Data protection law enforced with strong redressal mechanism
Phillipines- Datapoints:
- Data Privacy Act of 2012 (RA 10173): comprehensive, actively enforced privacy law with extraterritorial reach
- National Privacy Commission (NPC) oversees compliance; mandates 72-hour breach reporting for high-risk incidents
- NPC Advisory 2024-04 extends data privacy regulation to AI: requires Privacy Impact Assessments and 'meaningful human intervention' in AI-driven profiling and automated decision-making
- UNDP Digital Development Compass: Philippines scores 4 in data and privacy

Stage 1\n Greenfield\n (No Activity): No government AI initiatives or pilots
Stage 2\n Open to Adopt\n (Intent / Plan: Ad hoc exploratory pilots initiated by individual agencies
Stage 3\nEarly Success\n (Strategy/frame: Pilot AI projects implemented in select public-sector domains with defined scope
Stage 4\nMaturing\n(Implementation): Multiple AI deployments across ministries and public services; government AI programs with funding and targets
Stage 5\n Role Model\n (Leadership and: Large-scale AI deployment across government; national AI program with R&D, compute, and talent investment
Phillipines- Datapoints:
- National AI Center for Research and Innovation (NAICRI) launched February 2026 as the central shared high-performance computing (HPC) hub for government AI workloads
- Key sector deployments:
  a. Education: Project AGAP.AI (January 2026) — national AI upskilling program targeting 1.5 million learners and teachers
  b. Health: UTAK AI deployed in public hospitals for brain tumour detection; AINA tool delivers AI-driven nutrition assessments for rural health workers
  c. Disaster Resilience: AI-4RP (Project Gabay) — 2km resolution AI weather forecasting (DOST–Atmo Inc. partnership)
  d. Governance: Digital Bayanihan Chain (January 2026) — Philippines is the first country to record its entire national budget on a blockchain ledger
Insight?: The government recently launched the national AI hub called NAICRI, which provides the computing power needed for massive data projects. This means the expensive hardware is ready for use. UNDP can partner with the Department of Science and Technology to build a pipeline of projects that focus on the marginalised. Instead of just helping big business, we can use these supercomputers to solve real-world problems like predicting crop failures for small farmers or improving health services in rural areas.

Stage 1\n Greenfield\n (No Activity): No national DT strategy or roadmap
Stage 2\n Open to Adopt\n (Intent / Plan: Draft DT strategy or consultations underway
Stage 3\nEarly Success\n (Strategy/frame: National DT strategy formally published with implementation roadmap
Stage 4\nMaturing\n(Implementation): DT strategy actively implemented with targets, timelines, and dedicated budget
Stage 5\n Role Model\n (Leadership and: DT strategy mature, regularly updated, and used as a model for regional peers
Phillipines- Datapoints:
- E-Governance Act (RA 12254), signed September 2025: mandates full digitisation of the Philippine bureaucracy; institutionalises DICT as the lead digital authority; establishes a unified Integrated Government Network across all national and local agencies
- Konektadong Pinoy Act (RA 12234), August 2025: removes legislative franchise requirement for internet providers, enabling competitive entry (including Starlink) and expanded rural broadband
- Execution gap: 1,700+ Local Government Units (LGUs) must legally establish digital portals or adopt the DICT eLGU system by end-2026; many rural municipalities lack technical staff and budgets to comply
Insight?:
- The LGU compliance gap under RA 12254 is the most under-served entry point in the Philippines' digital transition. Over 1,700 LGUs must establish digital portals by end-2026, yet most lack technical staff and budgets. UNDP has a unique role providing 'last-mile' digital governance capacity building in high-need municipalities, where the World Bank and ADB have limited sub-national presence.
- UNDP can leverage its existing country office networks to co-design a standardised LGU Digital Readiness Toolkit — reducing reliance on bespoke and costly procurement processes.

Stage 1\n Greenfield\n (No Activity): No national digital ID system
Stage 2\n Open to Adopt\n (Intent / Plan: Digital ID system under design or piloting
Stage 3\nEarly Success\n (Strategy/frame: Operational national digital ID with basic authentication; enrollment underway
Stage 4\nMaturing\n(Implementation): Digital ID with biometric coverage, multi-factor authentication, and interoperability with at least one other DPI
Stage 5\n Role Model\n (Leadership and: Fully interoperable digital ID with high population coverage, privacy safeguards, and federated authentication
Phillipines- Datapoints:
Implementation status: Implemented
Program details: Philippine Identification System (PhilSys) | 2021 onwards
Implementation agency: Philippine Statistics Authority (PSA)
Adoption: 90 million registered Filipinos (~80% of population) as of December 2025
- Multi-modal biometrics (iris, fingerprint, facial capture) ensure unique, non-duplicable PhilSys Number (PSN) per citizen
- Interoperable with banking (instant e-KYC under BSP Circular 1105) and the eGovPH Super App (Single Sign-On for health, social, and local government services)

Stage 1\n Greenfield\n (No Activity): No national digital payments infrastructure
Stage 2\n Open to Adopt\n (Intent / Plan: Digital payments system in design or early pilot
Stage 3\nEarly Success\n (Strategy/frame: Operational digital payments system with basic interoperability
Stage 4\nMaturing\n(Implementation): Interoperable digital payments across financial institutions; growing financial inclusion; significant transaction volumes
Stage 5\n Role Model\n (Leadership and: Universal, fully interoperable digital payments; high financial inclusion; integrated with regional payment systems
Phillipines- Datapoints:
Implementation status: Implemented
Program details: PhilPaSSplus (real-time gross settlement system) | BSP | ISO 20022 compliant
Implementation agency: Bangko Sentral ng Pilipinas (BSP)
Adoption: PHP 151.3 trillion in transactions processed in Q3 2025 (21% increase year-on-year)
- Retail payment rails: InstaPay and PESONet for low-value real-time and high-value/batch transfers respectively
- System expanded toward 24/7 operations in 2026; underpins national wholesale CBDC pilot

Stage 1\n Greenfield\n (No Activity): No data exchange infrastructure or framework
Stage 2\n Open to Adopt\n (Intent / Plan: Data sharing framework under discussion or design
Stage 3\nEarly Success\n (Strategy/frame: Basic data exchange infrastructure operational; limited interoperability between agencies
Stage 4\nMaturing\n(Implementation): Cross-agency data exchange operational with consent mechanisms and defined standards
Stage 5\n Role Model\n (Leadership and: Mature data exchange ecosystem; extensive partner onboarding; privacy-preserving data sharing across sectors
Phillipines- Datapoints:
Implementation status: Implemented
Program details: eGovDX (Electronic Government Data Exchange) | DICT
Implementation agency: Department of Information and Communications Technology (DICT)
- Secure middleware enabling interoperable data sharing across national agencies via standardised APIs
- Operates under the Data Privacy Act (RA 10173); governed by Data Sharing Agreements (DSAs) and NPC notifications
- Active cross-agency use cases: DSWD–BSP social transfer targeting; PSA–BSP identity verification

Stage 1\n Greenfield\n (No Activity): No DPI assets operational; no use cases in public or private sector
Stage 2\n Open to Adopt\n (Intent / Plan: Use case of a DPI asset is being planned
Stage 3\nEarly Success\n (Strategy/frame: Use cases of DPI assets across 2–3 sectors in pilot or expansion stage
Stage 4\nMaturing\n(Implementation): Multiple use cases across 4+ sectors, cross-DPI integration in public service delivery
Stage 5\n Role Model\n (Leadership and: Use case deployment across all government schemes and private sector innovation
Phillipines- Datapoints:
- Social Protection (DSWD): 4Ps program serves 4.4 million+ households; fully digitised via PhilSys verification and PhilPaSSplus payment rails
- Health (PhilHealth): Insurance claims integrated with PhilSys registry for real-time eligibility checks and eClaims processing
- Government Finance (LandBank): Salaries, pensions, and social benefits processed via eMDS and Link.BizPortal digital rails
- Education (DepEd): Learner Information System (LIS) and Project Bukas use digital identifiers to track student progress across 1.5 million teachers and learners
- Cross-DPI (eGovPH Super App): Integrates 1,100+ government systems; 440 million+ transactions processed as of early 2026

Stage 1\n Greenfield\n (No Activity): Electricity access <70%;unreliable grid
Stage 2\n Open to Adopt\n (Intent / Plan: Electricity access <70%; national electrification roadmap or grid investment actively underway
Stage 3\nEarly Success\n (Strategy/frame: Electricity access >80%
Stage 4\nMaturing\n(Implementation): Electricity access >95%; stable grid nationally
Stage 5\n Role Model\n (Leadership and: Near-universal electricity supply
Phillipines- Datapoints:
- National household electrification rate: 89% by 2024. Expected to reach 91.7% (NEA and DOE, 2025)
- Regional disparity: Luzon at 98.5%, Visayas at 95.8%; Mindanao significantly lower at 83.8%, with BARMM provinces averaging below 50%

Stage 1\n Greenfield\n (No Activity): Internet penetration <70%; significant urban-rural gap
Stage 2\n Open to Adopt\n (Intent / Plan: Internet penetration <70%; active connectivity expansion program
Stage 3\nEarly Success\n (Strategy/frame: Internet penetration >80%
Stage 4\nMaturing\n(Implementation): Internet penetration >95%
Stage 5\n Role Model\n (Leadership and: Near-universal internet
Phillipines- Datapoints:
- 98 million internet users as of early 2026; 83.8% internet penetration rate (DataReportal, 2026)
- ~19 million people (16.2%) remain offline, primarily due to affordability constraints and regional infrastructure gaps

Stage 1\n Greenfield\n (No Activity): No cloud or data centre presence; no domestic hosting capacity
Stage 2\n Open to Adopt\n (Intent / Plan: Nascent or imported cloud services only; no domestic compute infrastructure
Stage 3\nEarly Success\n (Strategy/frame: Basic cloud or data hosting via domestic or regional providers
Stage 4\nMaturing\n(Implementation): Compute-capable national data centres operational; domestic and international cloud available
Stage 5\n Role Model\n (Leadership and: Availability of compute- govt effort, data centres
Phillipines- Datapoints:
- Installed data centre capacity: 632.80 MW across 28 operational colocation facilities (Mordor Intelligence, 2026)
- Google Cloud activated its first Philippine cloud region in early 2026; PLDT VITRO Sta. Rosa (50 MW) and STT Fairview (124 MW) are the country's first AI-ready hyperscale facilities
- NAICRI (launched February 2026) provides shared supercomputing (HPC) via DOST-ASTI COARE for government AI workloads

Stage 1\n Greenfield\n (No Activity): Large access gaps across geographic, gender, and socioeconomic dimensions; no disaggregated data collected
Stage 2\n Open to Adopt\n (Intent / Plan: Inclusion gaps identified and measured; pilot programmes or targeted interventions initiated
Stage 3\nEarly Success\n (Strategy/frame: Inclusion programmes operational;gaps narrowing with measurable progress
Stage 4\nMaturing\n(Implementation): Gaps significantly narrowed- rural-urban gap <5 percentage points, gender gap <5 percentage points; affrodable infrastructure available nationally
Stage 5\n Role Model\n (Leadership and: Near equitable access across all dimensions
Phillipines- Datapoints:
- National household internet access: 48.8% by July 2025; 47.5 percentage point gap between Metro Manila (68.7%) and Zamboanga Peninsula (21.2%)
- Gender parity: Philippines ranks 20th globally in the 2025 Global Gender Gap Report; near-equal internet usage between men and women
- Affordability remains primary barrier for 58.3% of unconnected households; average monthly internet spend: PHP 1,069 in 2024 (down from PHP 1,281 in 2019)
Insight?:
The massive 47.5 percentage point difference in internet access between Metro Manila and the Zamboanga Peninsula highlights a major inequality in the digital landscape of the Philippines. UNDP can address this by partnering with USAID BEACON and the Gates Foundation to fund community internet projects. This collaboration would avoid wasting resources and ensure that connectivity reaches the most isolated areas.

Even though men and women have similar access to technology, high costs remain a problem for 58 percent of households that are not yet online. UNDP can help the central bank and the telecommunications commission create digital subsidies for these families. These subsidies could be delivered through the same cash transfer system used by the 4Ps program to ensure help gets to those who need it most.

Sub-Parameter:
WB Worldwide Governance Indicators - Political Stability Score
2024
Stage 1\n Greenfield\n (No Activity): 0 – 20th percentile
Stage 2\n Open to Adopt\n (Intent / Plan: 20 – 40th percentile
Stage 3\nEarly Success\n (Strategy/frame: 40 – 60th percentile
Stage 4\nMaturing\n(Implementation): 60 – 80th percentile
Stage 5\n Role Model\n (Leadership and: 80 – 100th percentile
Phillipines- Datapoints: Political stability improved from 2019 to 2024 by ~4 percentage points (48.65 to 52.67) — Philippines sits within the 40–60th percentile of the WB Worldwide Governance Indicators

Sub-Parameter:
SK  - RECENT INDICATOR /Strategic and Long-term 
Subjective call - KIIs
Stage 1\n Greenfield\n (No Activity): High instability or conflict; digital transformation not a policy priority; no continuity across administrations
Stage 2\n Open to Adopt\n (Intent / Plan: Inconsistent policy continuity; digital agenda ad hoc or personality-driven; survives within one administration only
Stage 3\nEarly Success\n (Strategy/frame: Relative stability; digital transformation an articulated priority that survives at least one change of administration
Stage 4\nMaturing\n(Implementation): Digital transformation embedded in national agenda across multiple election cycles; stable institutional ownership
Stage 5\n Role Model\n (Leadership and: Digital transformation is a sovereign, cross-party strategic priority
Phillipines- Datapoints:
- E-Governance Act (RA 12254), signed September 2025: institutionalises digital transformation as state policy across all government branches — ensuring reform continuity beyond individual administrations
- DICT has navigated three presidential transitions since 2016; budget utilisation rose to 87% by late 2024
- USD 1.637 billion in World Bank digital financing locked in (USD 750M Second DPL 2024; USD 287M Digital Infrastructure Project) — acts as a financial anchor protecting the national fibre backbone
- Risk: UNESCO RAM (December 2025) warns that political tensions and the 2028 election cycle may delay enactment of 'hard laws' on AI governance

Stage 1\n Greenfield\n (No Activity): No designated lead agency for AI/DPI; no formal initiative; no cross-government coordination
Stage 2\n Open to Adopt\n (Intent / Plan: Single ministry or agency leading digital transformation; no cross-government coordination mechanism
Stage 3\nEarly Success\n (Strategy/frame: Inter-ministerial coordination mechanism established; lead agency with clear mandate
Stage 4\nMaturing\n(Implementation): Institutionalised inter-ministerial platforms with recurring mandates; regulatory sandboxes operational; CDO leading coordination
Stage 5\n Role Model\n (Leadership and: Whole-of-government architecture with clear roles across national and sector agencies; continuous, transparent coordination
Phillipines- Datapoints:
- DICT is the legally designated lead for digital transformation (RA 10844 and RA 12254)
- Key agencies under the digital governance mandate:
  1. Department of Information and Communications Technology (DICT) — lead authority
  2. National Privacy Commission (NPC) — data protection oversight
  3. Bangko Sentral ng Pilipinas (BSP) — fintech and digital payments regulation
  4. Philippine Statistics Authority (PSA) — PhilSys (Digital ID) management
- BSP Circular 1153: institutionalises a regulatory sandbox for fintech and AI service providers
- Gap: No single lead agency for AI governance as of 2026; LGU compliance gap persists (UNESCO RAM, December 2025)

Stage 1\n Greenfield\n (No Activity): No private sector engagement in digital agenda
Stage 2\n Open to Adopt\n (Intent / Plan: Bilateral engagement with select tech or telecom operators; largely ad hoc and commercial
Stage 3\nEarly Success\n (Strategy/frame: Structured consultations with private sector formally documented in strategy development; PPP frameworks emerging
Stage 4\nMaturing\n(Implementation): Formal public-private platforms operational; DPG and open-source ecosystem engaged; fintech and cloud providers integrated in delivery
Stage 5\n Role Model\n (Leadership and: Deep ecosystem integration; private sector co-invests in national digital infrastructure; DPGs and open standards embedded in public procurement
Phillipines- Datapoints:
- GCash (Mynt): 94 million registered users (81 million active) as of January 2025; primary vehicle for G2P disbursements — operates as de facto digital public infrastructure
- Microsoft: long-term MOU with DICT for government cloud (Azure); Google Cloud activated first on-shore Philippine region in early 2026
- Aboitiz Data Innovation (ADI): formal AI partnerships with DICT and PSA
- PLDT/Smart and Globe: active PPP frameworks under the Common Tower Policy for national connectivity
- DSWD piloted open-source Digital Public Goods (DPGs) for social protection in 2025; most other agencies remain on proprietary platforms — vendor lock-in risk noted

Stage 1\n Greenfield\n (No Activity): No MDB or bilateral engagement; no pipeline financing identified
Stage 2\n Open to Adopt\n (Intent / Plan: MDB/donor activity exploratory; primarily providing advisory or strategy support
Stage 3\nEarly Success\n (Strategy/frame: Recurrent MDB and bilateral investment in AI/DPI projects; approved and pipeline financing visible
Stage 4\nMaturing\n(Implementation): Multi-year strategic partnerships with ADB, World Bank, bilaterals operational
Stage 5\n Role Model\n (Leadership and: Development partners fully aligned to national roadmap
Phillipines- Datapoints:
- World Bank: USD 1.637 billion digital portfolio — USD 600M First DPL (2023), USD 750M Second DPL (November 2024), USD 287M Digital Infrastructure Project
- ADB: ProSPER Project (2026) — digital tools for agricultural value chains and logistics in Mindanao (Zamboanga Peninsula and BARMM)
- USAID BEACON: community network deployments in underserved areas
- Gates Foundation: grants via PSA to link PhilSys with digital financial services
- Philippines is an active member of the 50-in-5 Campaign — sharing PhilSys and open-source payment system roadmaps (MOSIP/Mojaloop) with 50 countries by 2028
Insight?:
The Philippines is an active leader in the 50-in-5 Campaign and plans to share its National ID and open-source payment systems with fifty countries by 2028. This provides a major opportunity for UNDP to act as a knowledge broker by facilitating learning exchanges between the Philippines and neighboring countries in the ASEAN and Pacific regions.

The large digital investments from the World Bank and the Asian Development Bank focus mainly on infrastructure and regional development. This creates a clear gap in funding for AI governance and the inclusion of civil society groups. UNDP is uniquely positioned to fill this void by providing technical advice and grant funding for ethics and community participation.

Stage 1\n Greenfield\n (No Activity): No domestic academic or research engagement in AI/DPI
Stage 2\n Open to Adopt\n (Intent / Plan: Isolated university or think tank activity; no formal linkage to government agenda
Stage 3\nEarly Success\n (Strategy/frame: At least one institution formally engaged in AI/DPI research; outputs informing policy discussions
Stage 4\nMaturing\n(Implementation): Multiple universities and think tanks contributing to AI/DPI policy; formal research-government linkage mechanisms
Stage 5\n Role Model\n (Leadership and: National research ecosystem; AI/DPI research institutions internationally recognised; research actively shaping national and regional policy
Phillipines- Datapoints:
- Ateneo Technical Resource Lab (TRL): launched early 2026 as a DPI testing hub focused on PhilSys integration, e-verify, and KYC analytics
- University of the Philippines: institutionalised 'UP Principles for Responsible AI' — serves as a national soft-law guide for government AI deployment
- DepEd–Microsoft E-CAIR (Education Centre for AI Research): AI-powered literacy tools scaled to millions of students and teachers
- DOST-ASTI COARE HPC facility: provides universities access to supercomputing resources for large-scale AI modelling and data-driven policy research

Stage 1\n Greenfield\n (No Activity): No civil society engagement; digital rights not on agenda
Stage 2\n Open to Adopt\n (Intent / Plan: Civil society informed but not consulted; awareness-level engagement only
Stage 3\nEarly Success\n (Strategy/frame: Civil society formally consulted on governance and ethics dimensions; inputs recorded and acknowledged
Stage 4\nMaturing\n(Implementation): Independent digital rights organisations active; civil society participates in governance and ethics discussions with formal channels
Stage 5\n Role Model\n (Leadership and: Civil society embedded in co-design and oversight; transparent public accountability mechanisms for AI and DPI governance
Phillipines- Datapoints:
- Foundation for Media Alternatives (FMA): lead digital rights watchdog; monitors DICT policy developments including social media verification and AI child protection debates
- Democracy.Net.PH: leading legislative push for the Magna Carta for Philippine Internet Freedom (MCPIF) — a crowdsourced, rights-based internet policy bill
- Civil society formally consulted in December 2025 multi-stakeholder process for the Implementing Rules of the E-Governance Act (RA 12254)
- Gap: PWD organisations, indigenous peoples, and informal sector workers systematically excluded from high-level AI and DPI consultations (UNESCO RAM, December 2025)

Stage 1\n Greenfield\n (No Activity): No dedicated AI/DPI budget line; digital transformation funded opportunistically or not at all
Stage 2\n Open to Adopt\n (Intent / Plan: Ministry nominally responsible; limited, non-recurrent annual allocation; insufficient for implementation
Stage 3\nEarly Success\n (Strategy/frame: Dedicated recurring domestic budget for AI/DPI development and upkeep; responsible ministry funded with multi-year commitment
Stage 4\nMaturing\n(Implementation): Sustained multi-year public investment programme; budget execution rates tracked and reported; domestic co-financing of externally-supported initiatives
Stage 5\n Role Model\n (Leadership and: Long-term sovereign funding mechanisms; significant R&D allocation; domestic digital transformation budget substantial and independently sustained
Phillipines- Datapoints:
- 2026 National Budget: PHP 6.793 trillion; DepEd received record PHP 992.7 billion allocation (includes 'future-ready' skills and computerisation)
- RA 12254 (E-Governance Act): mandates DICT to lead a Unified Project Management Office (UPMO); requires a Chief Information Officer (CIO) in every government agency; establishes 16 Regional Offices for digital service localisation
- Digital Bayanihan Chain (January 2026): entire 2026 national budget recorded on blockchain for fiscal transparency
- Gap: LGUs lack budget capacity and directives to align with national digitisation mandates; RA 9184 (Procurement Reform Act) cited as primary cause of underspending due to high bid failure rates (UNESCO RAM, December 2025)
